Notice Of Motion And Motion To Modify Judge Ulmer'S January 6, 2023 Order Denying The Motion For Summary Judgment Of Cahill Contractors Llc
Off Calendar Feb-07-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT CAHILL CONTRACTORS LLC'S Motion To Modify Judge Ulmer's January 6, 2023 Order Denying The Motion For Summary Judgment Of Cahill Contractors LLC

No appearance.

There being no appearance or objection, the court adopted its tentative ruling as follows:

The "motion to modify Judge Ulmer's January 6, 2023 order denying the motion for summary judgment of Cahill Contractors LLC" is denied.

The modification Cahill seeks is: "to clarify that Judge Ulmer's denial is without prejudice and that Cahill is permitted to refile a motion for summary judgment, arguing that the completed and accepted work doctrine bars its claims against Cahill Contractors, LLC with new and different facts." (Mot. 2:3-6.)

No modification or clarification is needed. Code of Civil Procedure 437c(f)(2) clearly contemplates a second summary judgment motion in which a party may establish "newly discovered facts or circumstances or a change of law supporting the issues reasserted in the summary judgment motion." Whether Cahill can carry that burden in a reasserted motion remains to be seen.

Counsel for the prevailing party shall prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported. (302/RBU)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-30-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT CAHILL CONTRACTORS LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT:

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Paul Matiasic, Esq. (415-675-1089; matiasic@mjlawoffice.com) of The Matiasic Firm, P.C. for Plaintiff.
Taylor Pohle, Esq. (626-243-1100; tpohle@ccllp.law) of Collins + Collins LLP for Defendant/Moving Party, Cahill Contractors LLC.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Cahill Contractors, LLC's motion to strike plaintiff's request for private attorney general fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 1021.5 is denied.

"Section 1021.5 permits a trial court to award attorney's fees to a successful party in any action that '(1) enforced an important public right, (2) conferred a significant public benefit, and (3) is of a type that private enforcement was necessary, and the financial burden justifies subsidizing the successful party's attorneys.'" (Burgess v. Coronado Unified Sch. Dist. (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 1, 7.)

The Court is dubious that plaintiff will ultimately be able to obtain the requested attorney's fee relief as plaintiff has a strong pecuniary interest in pursuing this case for her own benefit. (See Citizens for Amending Proposition L v. City of Pomona (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 1159, 1192-1193 ["[t]he necessity and financial burden requirement encompasses two issues: 'whether private enforcement was necessary and whether the financial burden of private enforcement warrants subsidizing the successful party's attorneys.'"]; Beach Colony II v. California Coastal Com. (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 106, 114 ["[Section 1021.5] was not designed as a method for rewarding litigants motivated by their own pecuniary interests who only coincidentally protect the public interest."].)

Nevertheless, the Court cannot conclude that plaintiff is not entitled to the requested fees as a matter of law merely based on the complaint. Defendant is ultimately raising factual issues and a more fully developed record regarding plaintiff's success, relief obtained, whether a public benefit is conferred, etc. is necessary to resolve the fee issue. The retrospective language of Burgess (e.g. "enforced" public right, "conferred" public benefit) suggests that the private attorney general fee issue is appropriately addressed at the end of the case.

Additional caselaw further suggests that this matter should not be resolved on the pleadings. "There is no requirement that the intent to seek attorney fees under section 1021.5 must be pleaded in the underlying action. [Citation.] Such fees are not part of the underlying cause of action, but are incidents to the cause and are properly awarded after entry of a ... judgment[.]" (Washburn v. City of Berkeley (1987) 195 Cal.App.3d 578, 583; see also Snatchko v. Westfield, LLC (2010) 187 Cal. App. 4th 469, 497 [same].)

Prevailing/Moving party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312 via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.
